enrichment.short_description: Open-source geocoding engine that turns OpenStreetMap data into address search and reverse geocoding, self-hosted or via a public API.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Nominatim is an open-source geocoding tool that uses OpenStreetMap data to find locations by name or address, and to reverse-geocode coordinates into the nearest address. It supports free-form queries as well as structured queries with specific fields like street, city, and country.

The software can be self-hosted at any scale, from a small laptop install covering a single region to a full planet-wide dataset, and it can incorporate near-real-time OpenStreetMap edits through minutely data updates. A public demo instance is also available for occasional, light use under the OpenStreetMap Foundation's Nominatim usage policy.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Nominatim suits developers and organizations that need address search or reverse geocoding inside their own applications without depending on a commercial geocoding API, particularly teams with privacy, cost, or data-sovereignty requirements that make self-hosting attractive.

features.title: Forward geocoding
features.description: Free-form or structured address and place-name search against OpenStreetMap data.

features.title: Reverse geocoding
features.description: Look up the nearest address or OSM object for a given set of coordinates.

features.title: Live OSM data
features.description: Can apply OpenStreetMap's minutely diffs so search results stay current.

features.title: Flexible scale
features.description: Runs on anything from a single laptop with a regional extract to a full planet import.

features.title: Public demo API
features.description: A hosted instance is available for occasional, low-volume use under OSMF's usage policy.

features.title: Multi-language place names
features.description: Returns localized place names where OSM data provides them.

pros: Free and fully open source
No usage caps when self-hosted on your own infrastructure
Near-real-time OpenStreetMap data updates
Scales from a single machine to a planet-wide dataset
Backed by the large OpenStreetMap community
cons: A full planet import requires significant server memory and disk space
The public demo API has strict rate limits and is not meant for production traffic
No official commercial support or SLA
Tuning search ranking and imports has a learning curve

faqs.question: Is Nominatim free?
faqs.answer: Yes. It's fully open source, and you can self-host it or use the public demo API for occasional use under its usage policy.

faqs.question: What data does it use?
faqs.answer: Nominatim geocodes exclusively from OpenStreetMap data.

faqs.question: Can I use the public API for production traffic?
faqs.answer: Only for light, infrequent use per the OSM Foundation's usage policy. Heavier production usage requires self-hosting your own instance.

faqs.question: Does Nominatim do routing?
faqs.answer: No, it only handles geocoding and reverse geocoding. Pair it with a routing engine like OSRM or OpenRouteService for directions.

faqs.question: What hardware does self-hosting require?
faqs.answer: A full planet install needs substantial RAM and disk space, while smaller regional extracts can run on much more modest hardware.

use_cases.title: Address search boxes
use_cases.description: Add address and place autocomplete search to a map-based application.

use_cases.title: Reverse geocoding for logistics
use_cases.description: Convert GPS coordinates from delivery or fleet data into readable addresses.

use_cases.title: Privacy-conscious geocoding
use_cases.description: Self-host geocoding to avoid sending address queries to third-party commercial APIs.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Nominatim is an open-source geocoding engine that searches and reverse-geocodes OpenStreetMap data, free to self-host or use via its public API.

enrichment.short_description: Self-hosted, open-source C++ routing engine that computes fast driving, cycling, and walking directions from OpenStreetMap data.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: OSRM is a modern C++ routing engine built for finding shortest paths across road networks. It's designed for speed, handling continental-sized road networks within milliseconds using contraction-hierarchy-based preprocessing.

It supports car, bicycle, and walking profiles that can be customized through Lua scripting, imports data directly from OpenStreetMap extracts, and exposes an HTTP API for routing, table (matrix), map matching, trip, and nearest-point queries. It's distributed under a permissive 2-clause BSD license and is commonly self-hosted via Docker.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: OSRM is aimed at developers building mapping, logistics, or fleet applications who need fast, self-hosted routing without relying on a commercial directions API.

features.title: High-performance routing
features.description: Computes routes across continental-scale networks in milliseconds using preprocessing algorithms.

features.title: Multiple transport profiles
features.description: Car, bicycle, and foot routing, customizable through Lua profile scripts.

features.title: OSM-based data import
features.description: Builds routing graphs directly from OpenStreetMap extracts.

features.title: HTTP API
features.description: JSON endpoints for routing, distance/time matrices, map matching, trip optimization, and nearest-point lookup.

features.title: Public demo
features.description: A live demo is available at map.project-osrm.org for quick testing.

features.title: Docker-friendly deployment
features.description: Commonly self-hosted with prebuilt Docker images.

pros: Extremely fast route computation at scale
Free under a permissive 2-clause BSD license
Actively maintained with a strong open-source community
Flexible transport profiles via Lua scripting
Well-documented HTTP API
cons: Self-hosting large regions or the full planet requires significant RAM and preprocessing time
No built-in map UI — developers must build their own frontend
No official commercial support or SLA
Turn-by-turn instruction quality is more basic than some commercial routing engines

faqs.question: Is OSRM free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's open source under a 2-clause BSD license.

faqs.question: What data source does it use?
faqs.answer: OpenStreetMap extracts.

faqs.question: Does OSRM support public transit routing?
faqs.answer: No, OSRM focuses on road-network routing for car, bike, and foot. For multimodal transit routing, use a tool like OpenTripPlanner.

faqs.question: Can I try it without installing anything?
faqs.answer: Yes, via the public demo at map.project-osrm.org.

faqs.question: What routing algorithms does it support?
faqs.answer: Contraction Hierarchies (CH) and Multi-Level Dijkstra (MLD), both preprocessing-based approaches for fast queries.

use_cases.title: In-app turn-by-turn directions
use_cases.description: Embed driving, cycling, or walking directions in a custom map application.

use_cases.title: Fleet route optimization
use_cases.description: Use the matrix and trip APIs to plan efficient multi-stop routes.

use_cases.title: Offline or air-gapped routing
use_cases.description: Self-host routing for environments where a third-party directions API isn't an option.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: OSRM is a free, open-source C++ routing engine that calculates fast car, bike, and foot directions from OpenStreetMap data, self-hosted via Docker.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source routing and geospatial analysis platform built on OpenStreetMap data, self-hostable or used via HeiGIT's hosted API.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: OpenRouteService is a geospatial routing and analysis platform built on OpenStreetMap data. It offers directions for cars, bicycles, pedestrians, and wheelchair users, along with isochrone (reachability) generation, many-to-many time/distance matrices, point-of-interest discovery, elevation data, and vehicle-routing optimization for fleets.

All server code is open source and available on GitHub for self-hosting, while a hosted API with a free tier and paid plans is operated by HeiGIT gGmbH (Heidelberg Institute for Geoinformation Technology), affiliated with Heidelberg University. Official SDKs are available for Python, R, JavaScript, and QGIS.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: It fits developers who need more than basic routing — isochrones, matrices, or elevation data — as well as humanitarian and academic teams that benefit from HeiGIT's mission-driven focus on open geospatial tools.

features.title: Multi-mode directions
features.description: Routing for car, bicycle, walking, and wheelchair-accessible profiles.

features.title: Isochrones
features.description: Generate reachability areas showing where you can travel within a given time or distance.

features.title: Matrix service
features.description: Compute many-to-many travel times and distances in a single request.

features.title: POI and geocoding endpoints
features.description: Look up points of interest and place names alongside routing.

features.title: Elevation data
features.description: Retrieve elevation profiles along a route.

features.title: Vehicle routing optimization
features.description: Solve multi-stop fleet routing problems.

features.title: Official SDKs
features.description: Client libraries for Python, R, JavaScript, and a QGIS plugin.

features.title: Self-hostable backend
features.description: The full routing engine is open source and can be deployed on your own infrastructure.

pros: Free tier available for small and moderate usage
Fully open-source backend that can be self-hosted
Backed by an academic institute with a humanitarian focus
Covers more than basic routing — isochrones, matrix, elevation, and optimization
Multiple official SDKs and a QGIS plugin
cons: The free hosted-API tier has daily request limits (for example, 500 isochrone requests per day)
Self-hosting global-scale data requires significant infrastructure
Paid plans are needed for high-volume production use of the hosted API
Documentation is spread across several sub-projects

faqs.question: Is OpenRouteService free?
faqs.answer: The backend is open source and free to self-host. The hosted API offers a free tier with daily request limits, plus paid plans for higher volume.

faqs.question: Who operates the hosted service?
faqs.answer: HeiGIT gGmbH, affiliated with Heidelberg University in Germany.

faqs.question: What data does it use?
faqs.answer: OpenStreetMap.

faqs.question: Can I self-host instead of using the hosted API?
faqs.answer: Yes, all server code is open source on GitHub for self-deployment.

faqs.question: Does it support wheelchair-accessible routing?
faqs.answer: Yes, a wheelchair routing profile is included alongside car, bike, and pedestrian profiles.

use_cases.title: Custom mapping apps
use_cases.description: Add routing, isochrones, or matrix calculations to a mapping application.

use_cases.title: Disaster-response analysis
use_cases.description: Use reachability and routing analysis for humanitarian planning.

use_cases.title: Fleet route optimization
use_cases.description: Plan efficient multi-stop delivery routes using the matrix and optimization services.

features: Higher request limits for production workloads

enrichment.seo_meta_description: OpenRouteService offers open-source routing, isochrones, and matrix APIs built on OpenStreetMap data, self-hostable or via HeiGIT's free/paid hosted API.

enrichment.short_description: Free, collaboratively-built map of the world with open data that anyone can use, edit, or build tools on top of.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: OpenStreetMap is a collaboratively-built mapping platform that provides map data for thousands of websites, mobile apps, and hardware devices. Volunteer contributors worldwide document roads, trails, cafes, railway stations, and countless other features using aerial imagery, GPS traces, and field verification.

The data is open and free to use under the Open Database License (ODbL), which requires attribution and share-alike terms for derivative databases. The project is operated on behalf of its community by the OpenStreetMap Foundation, and it underpins a large ecosystem of open-source geo tools, including geocoders, routers, and tile servers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: OpenStreetMap suits developers, researchers, and organizations that want free, downloadable map data without vendor lock-in, as well as anyone who wants to contribute local mapping knowledge to a shared public resource.

features.title: Free editable world map
features.description: Anyone can register and contribute edits to the map.

features.title: Open license data
features.description: Data is available under the ODbL, free to reuse with attribution.

features.title: Editing tools
features.description: Web-based (iD) and desktop (JOSM) editors for contributing map data.

features.title: Global coverage
features.description: Roads, buildings, points of interest, and trails maintained by a worldwide volunteer community.

features.title: API access
features.description: Programmatic access for querying and editing map data.

features.title: Ecosystem foundation
features.description: Powers third-party open-source tools like Nominatim, OSRM, OpenRouteService, and tile servers.

pros: Completely free and open data
Global community of active volunteer contributors
No vendor lock-in — data can be downloaded and self-hosted
Powers a large open-source geospatial tool ecosystem
Frequently updated, with minutely change feeds available
cons: Data completeness and accuracy vary by region
Building a full application requires separate tools for geocoding, routing, and tile rendering
Editing conventions have a learning curve for new contributors
No official SLA — governed by a volunteer community and the OpenStreetMap Foundation

faqs.question: Is OpenStreetMap free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes, the data is free under the Open Database License (ODbL), which requires attribution.

faqs.question: Who maintains OpenStreetMap?
faqs.answer: A global community of volunteer mappers, with the project operated on their behalf by the OpenStreetMap Foundation.

faqs.question: Can I download the map data?
faqs.answer: Yes, full-planet and regional data extracts are publicly downloadable.

faqs.question: Does OSM provide routing or geocoding itself?
faqs.answer: No, OSM provides the underlying map data. Separate open-source tools like OSRM, OpenRouteService, and Nominatim provide routing and geocoding on top of it.

faqs.question: Can businesses use OSM data commercially?
faqs.answer: Yes, under the ODbL's attribution and share-alike terms.

use_cases.title: Base map data for apps

use_cases.title: Self-hosted geo stacks
use_cases.description: Avoid proprietary map vendors by building an internal stack on OSM data.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: OpenStreetMap is a free, editable world map built by volunteers, offering open data under the ODbL license for any app or self-hosted geo stack.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source multimodal trip-planning server that combines transit, walking, cycling, and driving directions from OSM and GTFS data.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: OpenTripPlanner (OTP) is a collection of open-source software for multimodal trip planning and transportation analysis. It finds itineraries that combine transit, pedestrian, bicycle, and car segments through networks built from OpenStreetMap and GTFS transit data.

Since launching in 2009, OTP has attracted a thriving community of users and developers, with support from public agencies, startups, and transportation consultancies. It's typically self-hosted as a server, exposed through a web API, with JavaScript client libraries available for building web and mobile trip-planning interfaces.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: OTP is built for public transit agencies, transportation researchers, and developers who need to offer or analyze multimodal journey planning without relying on a proprietary trip-planning service.

features.title: Multimodal itinerary planning
features.description: Combines transit, walking, biking, and driving legs into a single trip.

features.title: GTFS and OSM ingestion
features.description: Builds its routing graph from standard transit (GTFS) and OpenStreetMap data.

features.title: Web API
features.description: Exposes trip-planning queries over a documented API.

features.title: JavaScript client libraries
features.description: Reference clients for integrating trip planning into web and mobile apps.

features.title: Used by transit agencies
features.description: Powers regional and national journey-planning services around the world.

features.title: Active open-source development
features.description: Regularly released and maintained by a community of contributors.

pros: Free and open source
Proven at scale by public transit agencies worldwide
Combines multiple transport modes in a single planner
Backed by an active, long-running developer community
Flexible self-hosted deployment
cons: Requires preparing and maintaining GTFS and OSM data inputs
Self-hosting needs familiarity with Java server infrastructure
No official hosted SaaS offering from the project itself
A production-ready frontend UI must be built separately or adapted from community clients

faqs.question: Is OpenTripPlanner free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's fully open source.

faqs.question: What data does it need?
faqs.answer: GTFS transit feeds and OpenStreetMap extracts for the area you want to cover.

faqs.question: Who uses OTP?
faqs.answer: Public transit agencies, transportation consultancies, researchers, and app developers worldwide.

faqs.question: Does it include a map UI?
faqs.answer: OTP provides an API and reference clients; most deployments build or customize a frontend on top of it.

faqs.question: When did the project launch?
faqs.answer: OpenTripPlanner launched in 2009.

enrichment.short_description: Self-hosted, open-source vector and raster map tile server for serving custom map styles from MBTiles and other tile sources.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: TileServer GL is a self-hosted tile server for serving rendered map tiles, vector tile styles, static map images, and WMTS endpoints. It's maintained under the MapTiler organization and is commonly deployed via Docker or npm.

It supports both vector-tile-only setups (via the lighter tileserver-gl-light variant) and full server-side raster rendering, making it a common choice for teams that want to host their own map styles instead of relying on a third-party map tile provider.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: It's aimed at developers and GIS teams who want to self-host custom map styles or serve pre-generated tiles for a web app, internal tool, or embedded device.

features.title: Vector and raster tile serving
features.description: Serves MapLibre/Mapbox GL vector styles as well as server-rendered raster tiles.

features.title: Static image generation
features.description: Render map snapshots server-side for a given location and style.

features.title: WMTS support
features.description: Exposes tiles via WMTS for compatibility with standard GIS clients.

features.title: Docker deployment
features.description: Official Docker images make self-hosting straightforward.

features.title: Lightweight variant
features.description: tileserver-gl-light serves vector tiles only, without raster rendering, for a smaller footprint.

features.title: Style and data source configuration
features.description: Manage map styles and tile data sources through configuration files.

pros: Free and open source
Simple Docker-based self-hosting
Supports both vector and raster tile workflows
Lightweight variant available for vector-only setups
Compatible with standard formats like MBTiles
cons: Requires pre-generated tile data (e.g., via tippecanoe or similar tools)
Documentation is fairly technical and developer-focused
No hosted SaaS offering from the project itself
Custom styling requires familiarity with the MapLibre/Mapbox GL style spec

faqs.question: Is TileServer GL free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's open-source software.

faqs.question: What tile formats does it support?
faqs.answer: MBTiles-based vector and raster tiles, along with WMTS endpoints.

faqs.question: Who maintains it?
faqs.answer: It's maintained under the MapTiler organization on GitHub.

faqs.question: Can I run it in Docker?
faqs.answer: Yes, an official Docker image is provided.

faqs.question: What's the difference between TileServer GL and the Light variant?
faqs.answer: The Light variant serves vector tiles only, without server-side raster rendering, for a smaller resource footprint.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, self-hosted GPS tracking platform for monitoring vehicles and devices in real time across hundreds of tracker protocols.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Traccar is a modern GPS tracking system for real-time location monitoring of vehicles, personal devices, and other tracked assets. It supports a very wide range of GPS tracker hardware through a large library of communication protocols.

The core server is free and open source, with installers and source code available for self-hosting on Windows, Linux, and other platforms. Traccar also offers an optional hosted cloud account for users who don't want to manage their own server infrastructure.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Traccar fits small fleet operators, logistics teams, and individuals who want real-time tracking, geofencing, and reporting for vehicles or devices, whether self-hosted for full control or via a hosted account for convenience.

features.title: Real-time GPS tracking
features.description: Live map view of all connected tracking devices.

features.title: Broad protocol support
features.description: Compatible with a very wide range of GPS tracker hardware and brands.

features.title: Geofencing and alerts
features.description: Push and email notifications for events like geofence breaches, harsh driving, or maintenance reminders.

features.title: Reporting
features.description: Location history, trip analysis, route replay, and summary reports.

features.title: Web admin interface
features.description: Browser-based dashboard for managing devices, users, and geofences.

features.title: Mobile client apps
features.description: Official Traccar Client apps let a phone act as a GPS tracker.

pros: Free and open source to self-host
Extremely broad device and protocol compatibility
Real-time tracking with geofencing and alerting
Long-running, actively maintained project (since 2009)
Optional hosted cloud account for those who don't want to self-host
cons: Full support and some conveniences are tied to the hosted cloud plans
Self-hosting requires ongoing server maintenance
Interface is functional but less polished than some commercial fleet platforms
Configuring specific device protocols can require technical know-how

faqs.question: Is Traccar free?
faqs.answer: The core server software is free and open source to self-host. A hosted cloud account is also offered as an alternative to self-hosting.

faqs.question: What devices does it support?
faqs.answer: Traccar supports a very wide range of GPS tracker hardware through its large library of communication protocols.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Traccar?
faqs.answer: Yes, it runs on Windows, Linux, and other platforms, with installers and source code on the official site.

faqs.question: Does Traccar have mobile apps?
faqs.answer: Yes, official Traccar Client apps are available for using a phone as a tracker.

faqs.question: How long has Traccar existed?
faqs.answer: Since 2009.

enrichment.short_description: Minimalist self-hosted map tracker and trip planner for visualizing points of interest and organizing multi-day trip itineraries.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: TRIP is a self-hosted application that combines a minimalist point-of-interest (POI) map tracker with a trip planner, letting you visualize places you care about and organize the details of an upcoming trip.

The project emphasizes privacy, with no telemetry, tracking, or ads, and is deployed through Docker using a provided docker-compose configuration. It's completely open source, with documentation maintained by its developer, itskovacs.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: TRIP suits travelers and self-hosting enthusiasts who want a lightweight, privacy-respecting alternative to commercial trip-planning apps for tracking places and planning itineraries with travel companions.

enrichment.short_description: Self-hosted, open-source Docker tool that downloads Spotify playlists and tracks as tagged audio files.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Downtify is a self-hosted music downloader that takes a Spotify link and produces a tagged audio file, without requiring API keys or a Spotify account. It reads the metadata Spotify exposes on its public embed pages, finds the closest matching audio on YouTube Music, and hands the file to yt-dlp and ffmpeg for download, with tags written by mutagen.

The whole pipeline runs in a single Docker container. Features include a Playlist Monitor that automatically downloads new songs added to watched playlists, support for MP3, FLAC, M4A, OGG, and OPUS formats, embedded plain and time-synced lyrics from lrclib, a built-in web player, and M3U export compatible with self-hosted media servers like Jellyfin, Navidrome, and Plex. The project's own documentation states it does not support unauthorized downloading of copyrighted material and takes no responsibility for user actions.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Downtify is aimed at self-hosted media enthusiasts who already run tools like Jellyfin, Navidrome, or Plex and want a way to populate a personal library from Spotify playlists.

features.title: Spotify link input
features.description: Paste a track, album, or playlist link without needing API keys or a Spotify account.

features.title: YouTube Music matching
features.description: Finds the closest matching audio via yt-dlp based on Spotify's public metadata.

features.title: Metadata tagging
features.description: Writes proper tags and album art using mutagen.

features.title: Multiple audio formats
features.description: Supports MP3, FLAC, M4A, OGG, and OPUS at a chosen bitrate.

features.title: Synced lyrics
features.description: Fetches and embeds plain and time-synced lyrics from lrclib.

features.title: Playlist Monitor
features.description: Automatically downloads new songs added to watched Spotify playlists.

features.title: Web player and M3U export
features.description: Built-in playback plus M3U playlist export for Jellyfin, Navidrome, and Plex.

pros: Free and open source
No Spotify API keys or account required
Supports multiple audio formats and embedded synced lyrics
Playlist Monitor automates downloads of new tracks
Exports playlists compatible with popular self-hosted media servers
cons: The legal status of downloading copyrighted music this way is ambiguous and use is at the user's own risk
Audio quality depends on the closest match found on YouTube Music rather than the original source file
Small independent project without formal support
Requires Docker for deployment

faqs.question: Is Downtify legal to use?
faqs.answer: The project's own documentation states it does not support unauthorized downloading of copyrighted material and takes no responsibility for user actions — check your local laws and Spotify's terms before using it.

faqs.question: Do I need a Spotify account or API key?
faqs.answer: No, Downtify reads public metadata from Spotify's embed pages without requiring credentials.

faqs.question: What audio formats are supported?
faqs.answer: MP3, FLAC, M4A, OGG, and OPUS.

faqs.question: Can it monitor playlists for new songs automatically?
faqs.answer: Yes, via the Playlist Monitor feature.

faqs.question: Does it work with Jellyfin, Navidrome, or Plex?
faqs.answer: Yes, it can export M3U playlists that those media servers pick up automatically.

use_cases.title: Feeding a self-hosted media server

use_cases.title: Archiving playlists with lyrics
use_cases.description: Keep a personal archive of playlists including synced lyrics.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Downtify is a free, open-source, self-hosted Docker tool for downloading Spotify playlists as tagged MP3/FLAC/OGG files with synced lyrics.

enrichment.short_description: Open-source, self-hosted music collection manager that automates monitoring, downloading, and organizing albums via Usenet and BitTorrent.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Lidarr is a music collection manager for Usenet and BitTorrent users. It monitors RSS feeds for new album releases from artists you follow, then interfaces with the download clients and indexers you configure to retrieve, organize, and rename music files, including automatically upgrading existing library files when higher-quality versions become available.

It's part of the wider *arr family of automation tools (alongside Sonarr and Radarr) and is open source, running on Windows, Linux, macOS, Docker, BSD, and Synology NAS devices with a web-based management interface.

long_form_blocks.heading: Who It's For
paragraphs: Lidarr suits home server and NAS users who already use Usenet or torrent indexers and download clients and want to automate music library growth, tagging, and organization rather than managing it manually.

features.title: Automated release monitoring
features.description: Watches RSS feeds for new releases from followed artists.

features.title: Indexer and download client integration
features.description: Works with configured Usenet and BitTorrent indexers and download clients.

features.title: Automatic import and renaming
features.description: Organizes and renames files according to a configurable naming scheme.

features.title: Quality profiles and upgrades
features.description: Automatically upgrades library files to better quality when available.

features.title: Calendar view
features.description: Tracks upcoming album releases.

features.title: Import lists
features.description: Syncs followed artists from services like Last.fm.

features.title: Cross-platform support
features.description: Runs on Windows, Linux, macOS, Docker, BSD, and Synology NAS.

pros: Free and open source
Automates music library growth, tagging, and organization
Broad platform support, including Docker and NAS
Integrates with the wider *arr ecosystem (Sonarr, Radarr, Prowlarr)
Actively developed with a large community
cons: Requires separately configured indexers and download clients to be useful
Initial setup and configuration has a learning curve
No official mobile app
Legal considerations depend entirely on the indexers and content sources you use

faqs.question: Is Lidarr free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it's free and open source.

faqs.question: Does Lidarr download music itself?
faqs.answer: No, it automates the process by interfacing with separate download clients and indexers that you configure.

faqs.question: What platforms does Lidarr run on?
faqs.answer: Windows, Linux, macOS, Docker, BSD, and Synology NAS.

faqs.question: Does Lidarr work with Usenet and torrents?
faqs.answer: Yes, both are supported through configurable indexers and download clients.

faqs.question: Can Lidarr upgrade existing files automatically?
faqs.answer: Yes, using customizable quality profiles.

use_cases.title: Automated music library
use_cases.description: Automate growing and organizing a personal music library alongside Usenet or torrent clients.

use_cases.title: Library organization
use_cases.description: Tag and organize an existing large music collection.

use_cases.title: Full *arr automation stack
use_cases.description: Pair with Sonarr, Radarr, and Prowlarr for a complete home media automation setup.

enrichment.seo_meta_description: Lidarr is a free, open-source, self-hosted music collection manager that automates monitoring, downloading, and organizing albums via Usenet/BitTorrent.
